museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: Space Invaders gains an official follow-up in Japan according to several sources, while the original game continues to define what an arcade hit looks like.
museum.curatorNote: Space Invaders Part II is date-tricky: sources differ between July and September for Japan, with North American debut later.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: Five researched moments from a year where arcade, home-computer and magazine evidence rarely lines up like a modern launch calendar.

items.title: Space Invaders Part II appears in the record
items.body: Several sources place Taito's Space Invaders Part II in Japan in July, though others cite September. The safer reading is summer 1979 Japan, with North America later.
items.sourceNote: Wikipedia-style sources cite July; Arcade-History cites September.

items.title: The sequel is still built from the original invasion
items.body: Part II reused and extended the Space Invaders formula rather than replacing it, showing how strong the original cabinet economy remained.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders Part II development summaries.

items.title: Operators want familiar earners
items.body: A sequel to a proven cabinet was less risky than an unknown form. 1979 arcade innovation lived beside careful imitation.

items.title: UK players know the invasion even when dates blur
items.body: The original Space Invaders had already reached enormous UK visibility; the sequel story was part of invasion culture rather than a neat day-one event.
items.sourceNote: Space Invaders sources estimate 85,000 UK machines by end of 1979.

items.title: Home play still cannot match the cabinet
items.body: Before the 1980 Atari VCS port, the official home Space Invaders experience is still something players are waiting for.
items.sourceNote: Atari VCS Space Invaders port is a 1980 release.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: A deliberately sparse shelf: only month-specific releases supported by the sources reviewed are displayed.

items.title: Space Invaders Part II
items.body: Taito's official sequel added new touches to the alien formation. The exact Japanese month is disputed, so this entry avoids a fake exact date.
items.sourceNote: Wikipedia-style sources cite July; Arcade-History cites September; North American debut is usually placed in November before 1980 mass production.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: Four hardware/context objects explaining the month without pretending Britain had a mature games retail shelf yet.

items.title: Atari VCS as the home reference point
items.body: In July 1979 the Atari VCS was not yet the renamed 2600, but it was becoming shorthand for home videogames in North America. In Britain it remained aspirational, expensive, and less ordinary than the arcade.
items.sourceNote: Atari Archive late-1979 context notes Atari sold roughly 600,000 VCS units over 1979 and passed one million installed.

items.title: Arcade cabinets as the premium display
items.body: The most advanced game experiences were still cabinets: dedicated controls, loud sound, glowing marquees and operators choosing what deserved floor space.
items.sourceNote: Used as period arcade context around Space Invaders, Lunar Lander, Galaxian and Asteroids.

items.title: Home computers before the UK bedroom boom
items.body: The Apple II, PET, TRS-80, Nascom and other early machines made games programmable, but they were not yet the cheap British home-micro explosion of 1982.
items.sourceNote: Supported by Practical Computing, PCW, BYTE and Creative Computing context.

items.title: Black-and-white and colour lived together
items.body: This was a transition year. Some games still relied on monochrome displays, overlays or stark vector lines, while titles such as Galaxian made colour feel increasingly important.
items.sourceNote: Galaxian and Lunar Lander sources support the display-technology contrast.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: In 1979, the magazine was often the map: part catalogue, part classroom, part rumour network.

items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed placeholder for Practical Computing, July 1979.
items.curatorNote: Practical Computing belongs to the 1979 map of play: not always a games magazine, but part of the paper network that taught people what small computers and arcade culture meant.
items.body: Practical Computing belongs to the 1979 map of play: not always a games magazine, but part of the paper network that taught people what small computers and arcade culture meant.
items.certainty: publication context verified; cover image reconstructed placeholder

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Online play existed at the edge of institutions and networks, but most players lived in an offline culture of paper, coins and local knowledge.

items.title: Online life was mostly not a visitor experience yet
items.body: For most players in July 1979, games were discovered through arcades, shops, magazines, clubs and word of mouth. The web did not exist, and domestic online play was not part of ordinary gaming life.
items.sourceNote: Included to prevent projecting later online habits back onto 1979.

items.title: MUD1 existed, but behind institutional doors
items.body: Roy Trubshaw and Richard Bartle's MUD work at the University of Essex belongs to this era, but it was a university/mainframe culture, not something a high-street player would casually log into at home.
items.sourceNote: MUD1 sources place creation in 1978 and later network significance through Essex connections.

items.title: Printed listings were the practical network
items.body: For home-computer owners, the closest thing to downloading a game was often typing a listing from a magazine, saving it to cassette, and hoping the checksum of human patience held.
items.sourceNote: Based on Creative Computing, BYTE, Practical Computing and similar magazine culture.

items.title: The arcade was a live feed
items.body: Scores, tricks and rumours updated by watching other people play. A good cabinet in a cafe, arcade or seaside amusement hall could teach faster than any printed manual.
items.sourceNote: Period arcade culture context rather than a single dated claim.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: A month from the pre-specialist-games-magazine world: public cabinets, expensive machines and a future still forming.

items.title: The future was in public
items.body: The future was still public: a glowing cabinet, someone waiting behind you, and a score that mattered because other people could see it.

items.title: The home version was still an idea
items.body: At home, games were mixed with education, programming and the awkward argument that an expensive computer was not only a toy.

items.title: Britain felt it through arcades and magazines
items.body: In Britain the texture was arcades, computing magazines, shop-window machines and rumours rather than a mature boxed-games retail culture.

items.title: Dates were not launch-day culture yet
items.body: The modern habit of exact worldwide release dates does not fit 1979 neatly. Games appeared through trade shows, operator routes, regional shipments, adverts and later memory.

Magazine cover images are reconstructed placeholders and should be replaced with verified/licensed scans if used editorially.
